explanation.title: Putting It All Together: Build Your Personal Portfolio
explanation.content:
# The Mini Project

You have now learned every fundamental skill in HTML and CSS:

1. **HTML structure** — DOCTYPE, head, body, metadata
2. **Text elements** — headings, paragraphs, lists, inline tags
3. **Links and images** — anchors, img, tables, forms
4. **CSS selectors** — element, class, ID, pseudo-class, descendant
5. **Box model** — margin, padding, border, box-sizing
6. **Typography and colour** — font stacks, colours, CSS variables
7. **Flexbox** — axes, alignment, flex-wrap, flex-grow
8. **Responsive design** — media queries, mobile-first, fluid units
9. **Semantic HTML** — header, nav, main, article, section, footer

In this lesson, you will build a **personal portfolio website** — the most useful project a beginner can make. Employers and clients look at portfolios before deciding whether to hire someone.

## Portfolio Structure

A minimal portfolio has four sections:

```
header  ← your name + nav links
hero    ← your headline + one-sentence description
projects ← 3-4 cards, each with a title, description, and link
contact ← email link or a form
footer  ← copyright + social links
```

## What Makes a Good Portfolio?

- **One clear h1** — your name or professional title
- **A specific description** — not "I am a developer" but "I build Python and JavaScript web apps"
- **Real project links** — even small projects count
- **Clean, readable design** — white space is your friend
- **Mobile responsive** — recruiters check on phones

## Deploying Your Portfolio

GitHub Pages is the fastest free hosting for HTML/CSS sites:

1. Create a repository named `yourusername.github.io`
2. Push your `index.html` and `style.css` files
3. Your site is live at `https://yourusername.github.io`

Netlify and Vercel are also free and require no Git knowledge — drag and drop your folder.

In the code example below, you will see a complete, fully styled, responsive portfolio page using every concept from this course. Study the structure carefully — your exercise is to personalise and extend it.

codeExample.title: Complete Personal Portfolio
codeExample.code:
Priya Verma
Skills Projects

Priya Verma

Full-Stack Developer — Python & JavaScript

View Work

Skills

HTML CSS JavaScript Python

Projects

Task Manager

Full-stack to-do app.

View →

Weather App

Live weather data.

View →

Portfolio

Built with HTML & CSS.

Source →

© 2025 Priya Verma
codeExample.explanation:
- `position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 10` on `header` — the nav sticks to the top while the user scrolls through the page
- `clamp(24px, 5vw, 44px)` on `h1` — the name scales fluidly between phone and desktop sizes
- Anchor links like `href="#projects"` — jump to the element with `id="projects"` on the same page
- `flex: 0 0 calc(33.333% - 14px)` for project cards — three columns with 20px gap compensation
- Mobile breakpoints convert the three-column grid to two columns at 768px and one column at 480px

exercise.title: Personalise the Portfolio
exercise.instructions:
Take the portfolio starter and personalise it: change the name in header strong and h1 to your name, update the role paragraph to your actual role or aspiration, replace the four skill span tags with your real skills, update the three project cards with your own project names and descriptions, and update the contact email href to your address. Keep all HTML structure and CSS unchanged.

Expected: your name visible, four skill tags, three project cards.
exercise.starterCode:
Your Name Here
SkillsProjects

Your Name Here

Your role or aspiration here

Skill 1 Skill 2 Skill 3 Skill 4

© 2025 Your Name
exercise.solutionCode:
Rahul Sharma
SkillsProjects

Rahul Sharma

Python and JavaScript Developer from Delhi

HTML CSS Python JavaScript

© 2025 Rahul Sharma
hints: 💡 Replace 'Your Name Here' in the header strong tag and h1 tag with your actual name.
💡 Replace each 'Skill N' span text with a real skill you have or want to learn.
💡 Update the footer copyright line to include your name instead of 'Your Name'.

questions.question: What is the fastest free way to host a personal HTML/CSS website publicly?
options: Buying a server and installing Apache
Emailing the HTML file to visitors
Using GitHub Pages, Netlify, or Vercel — all support static HTML for free
Uploading to Google Drive and sharing the link
questions.explanation: GitHub Pages, Netlify, and Vercel all offer free hosting for static websites (pure HTML, CSS, and JavaScript). GitHub Pages is set up by creating a repository named yourusername.github.io. Netlify and Vercel support drag-and-drop deployment — no terminal required. All three provide HTTPS automatically.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most important thing to include on a developer portfolio?
options: Actual links to real projects you have built
A long list of every technology you have heard of
questions.explanation: Employers and clients hire based on what you can build, not what you claim. Links to real projects — even small ones — are far more convincing than a list of skills. A project link proves ability; a skill tag only makes a claim. Start with one or two genuine projects rather than an impressive-looking but empty portfolio.

questions.question: What do the six concepts HTML, CSS selectors, box model, Flexbox, media queries, and semantic HTML have in common?
options: They are all optional features only used by advanced developers
They are the complete foundation needed to build any static webpage
They only apply to mobile development
They must be learned in a different order than listed
questions.explanation: These six areas form the complete foundation of static web development. HTML provides structure, CSS selectors target elements, the box model controls spacing and sizing, Flexbox handles layout, media queries add responsiveness, and semantic HTML adds meaning and accessibility. With these six skills you can build any website that does not require a server or database.
